perturbation.original_text: 6. The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser. The Recipient may retain copies for internal archiving purposes indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text allows the Recipient to retain copies of Confidential Information indefinitely for internal archiving. This contradicts the principle of confidentiality and potentially breaches data protection laws, such as GDPR (if the information contains personal data).
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR requires personal data to be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Retaining copies indefinitely for archiving violates this principle if the Confidential Information contains personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Recipient to retain copies of Confidential Information indefinitely for internal archiving purposes. The Law Explanation states that Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR requires personal data to be kept only as long as necessary. The modified text therefore contradicts GDPR if the Confidential Information contains personal data. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is 'None', Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'UK GDPR guidance and resources' suggesting relevance to GDPR laws. The indefinite retention permitted in the changed text conflicts with GDPR's requirement to keep data only as long as necessary, establishing a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advi sers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the Discloser and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 2 above and this clause 3.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party. The Recipient shall not be liable for any breach of confidentiality caused by a cyberattack or data breach, regardless of the Recipient's security measures.
perturbation.explanation: This modification exempts the Recipient from liability for confidentiality breaches caused by cyberattacks, regardless of their security measures. This contradicts the requirement for 'appropriate technical and organisational measures' under GDPR (if the information contains personal data) and the common law duty of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR requires controllers (the Recipient, in this case) to implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. A blanket exemption from liability for cyberattacks, regardless of security measures, violates this requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Article III | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Primary tabs Section 1. The judicial power of the United States, shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The judges, both of the supreme and inferior courts, shall hold their offices during good behaviour, and shall, at stated times, receive for their services, a compensation, which shall not be diminished during their continuance in office. Section 2. The judicial power shall extend to all cases, in law and equity, arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, and treaties made, or which shall be made, under their authority;--to all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls;--to all cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction;--to controversies to which the United States shall be a party;--to controversies between two or more states;-- between a state and citizens of another state ;--between citizens of different states;--between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states, and between a state, or the citizens thereof, and foreign states, citizens or subjects. In all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, and those in which a state shall be party, the Supreme Court shall have original jurisdiction. In all the other cases before mentioned, the Supreme Court shall have appellate jurisdiction, both as to law and fact, with such exceptions, and under such regulations as the Congress shall make. The trial of all crimes, except in cases of impeachment, shall be by jury; and such trial shall be held in the state where the said crimes shall have been committed; but when not committed within any state, the trial shall be at such place or places as the Congress may by law have directed. Section 3. Treason against the United States, shall consist only in levying war against them, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. No person shall be convicted of treason unless on the testimony of two witnesses to the same overt act, or on confession in open court. The Congress shall have power to declare the punishment of treason, but no attainder of treason shall work corruption of blood, or forfeiture except during the life of the person attainted. ‹ Article II up Article IV › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text exempts the recipient from liability for confidentiality breaches caused by cyberattacks, regardless of security measures. This directly contradicts the GDPR requirement (as highlighted in the Law Explanation) for implementing 'appropriate technical and organizational measures' to ensure data security. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 relates to the U.S. Constitution and doesn't directly apply, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both strongly indicate a violation of GDPR's data security mandates due to the blanket exemption, making the contract non-compliant with data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be c onstrued in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non- exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this agreement shall be resolved through mandatory, binding arbitration in a jurisdiction selected by the Discloser, waiving the right to court proceedings.
perturbation.explanation: Mandatory, binding arbitration clauses, especially those unilaterally chosen by one party, can be challenged under English law if they are deemed unfair or contravene consumer protection legislation (if the Recipient is a consumer).
perturbation.law_explanation: Part 1, Chapter 4 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 addresses unfair contract terms. A clause forcing a consumer to resolve disputes through arbitration in a jurisdiction chosen solely by the business, waiving court access, is likely to be considered unfair and unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces mandatory, binding arbitration, unilaterally chosen by the Discloser, effectively waiving the right to court proceedings. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that this contradicts consumer protection laws and is likely to be considered unfair and unenforceable under the Consumer Rights Act 2015. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not found' error, but the absence of contradicting information does not negate the potential violation, and the remaining information indicates a probable legal contradiction. The change moves from non-exclusive jurisdiction of English courts to mandatory arbitration, a significant and direct alteration with potential legal ramifications.
